title_en: Back to Being an Overseas Address Hopper After Half a Year
excerpt_en: 2024/10/20 It's been exactly 12 weeks since I came to Ireland. And at this timing, my housing contract has ended. I found the place I've been living in through Airbnb, so I decided to live there for one month without even viewing it first.
body_en:
2024/10/20

It's been exactly 12 weeks since I came to Ireland.

And at this timing, my housing contract has ended.

I found the place I've been living in through Airbnb, so

I decided to live there for one month without even viewing it first,

and since I chose it based on the cheap price and its relatively close proximity to the city, the quality wasn't all that great.

Still, since it was a share house,

there was surprisingly a lot of interaction,

and it was fun in its own way.

That said, I didn't talk about my work at all.

There were no personal lockers,

and if I mentioned that I work as a photographer,

people would know I have cameras,

and if they got stolen, it'd be game over.

*Just as a precaution

So it turned out to be a great opportunity to experience my first overseas share house.

And today being move-out day,

I packed up my stuff and headed out!

From here, I'll be a traveler for a month, so

I currently have no home.

Starting tomorrow, I'll be doing activities in Estonia for a week.

However, I'm not taking my huge suitcase to Estonia. There's just too much unnecessary stuff in it.

Basically, for short-term trips to other countries, I travel with just a backpack and a tote bag, and I wanted to do the same this time, but

since I have no home,

Taiki-san, who I've become friends with since coming here,

agreed to keep my suitcase at his place!

On top of that, after I return from Estonia, I'll be in Ireland during the Halloween period, and during that time, I'm planning to stay at Taiki-san's place.

*Freeloading

For someone like me who gives up their home

both in Japan and overseas,

having someone hold onto my luggage

and even letting me stay for a short period

is truly something I'm grateful for.

What I'm doing is

not much different from my activities in Japan, haha.

*3 years as an address hopper

So after dropping off my luggage,

the two of us headed straight to

a Sunday Japanese meetup event.

Since there were a few people I knew,

after chatting with them,

it was time to socialize with Japanese members who've been in Dublin for a long time.

This was also a very meaningful time.

And then, right after that,

I went out for wine with Taiki-san.

Since coming to Dublin,

I haven't been particular about food at all,

but that's only when I'm eating alone --

when I'm eating with someone, I eat properly.

I handled the wine selection,

and left the food choices up to him.

It's always like this, haha.

And after dinner was over, we parted ways.

Since I'm flying to Estonia tomorrow morning,

I headed straight to the airport.

*I don't have a home anymore, so

Of course, there were no beds,

but since I arrived a bit early, I grabbed a chair

and spent the night there.

At 4 AM, I passed through security,

and since I have a Priority Pass,

I headed to the lounge.

You can get a delicious breakfast for free,

so it's incredibly comfortable, haha.

I spent about 2 hours there,

and then it was time to fly!!

This time I'm also using

Ryanair, Ireland's LCC.

Round trip is 14,000 yen, so

going from Ireland to other countries is a great deal.

So with that,

I'm off to Estonia!!

It's a short one week, but

I want to make it a meaningful time full of learning!

excerpt_en: So, I've come from Ireland to Estonia! I'll be doing activities in Tallinn, the capital of Estonia, for about a week. Here are the objectives for my activities in Estonia: Expanding the FOMUS brand, increasing the number of people who resonate with MaSU,
body_en:
So, I've come from Ireland

to Estonia!

I'll be doing activities in Tallinn, the capital of Estonia,

for about a week.

Here are the objectives for my activities in Estonia:

  1. Expanding the FOMUS Brand
  • Increase the number of people who resonate with "MaSU" and spread the brand.
  1. Building Connections
  • Connect with Japanese people living in Estonia, Estonian models, and business owners.
  1. Sightseeing and Inspiration
  • Enjoy the scenery of Tallinn's Old Town and use it for building KUKU's world.

*KUKU is FOMUS's official character.

  1. Learning
  • Learn about Estonia's "e-government" system.

Learn about life in Estonia.

  1. Finding Models
  • Make friends at bars and pubs and use that as an opportunity to find models.
  1. Finding Investors
  • Find investors who will fund the FOMUS and KUKU projects.
  1. Cultural Experience
  • Experience saunas and spas and understand Estonian culture.
  1. ** Masu Photo Photobook in Estonia

Gather models and create a photobook in Estonia.

Roughly, that's the plan,

and the difficulty ranges from 1 to 10.

Since I only have one week,

I plan to do as much as I can.

Naturally,

I have no connections in Estonia either, so

I have no choice but to expand through human connections.

And just like that, I arrived in Tallinn!

After a roughly 30-minute bus ride, I was in the city.

From there, I walked about 10 minutes to

a hostel inside Tallinn's Old Town.

This time I'm planning to stay at two places,

with the first base being four nights.

Even so, it's only about 9,000 yen -- super cheap!

It's a dormitory room.

Rather than splurging on a hotel,

I prefer dormitory-style hostels where

I can interact with as many people as possible,

and it saves on accommodation costs too, so

I prefer booking hostels.

After checking in and leaving my luggage,

it was time to go sightseeing!

Since today is arrival day,

I have no scheduled plans, so

I'll be sightseeing while

scouting locations for photo shoots.

So I was heading out to explore the city,

but I was already hungry, so

I checked the hostel's recommended gourmet MAP

and headed to a nearby kebab shop.

I had a delicious kebab.

And it was less than 4 euros, so it's a great deal!

After filling up,

I went exploring the city!

Tallinn's Old Town is relatively compact,

so I was able to visit

most of the famous tourist spots.

Also, Estonia is famous for amber, and

there are amber shops everywhere.

About Amber

Amber is a natural organic mineral formed from tree resin that has fossilized over long periods of time. The resin, mainly secreted from pine and cedar trees, became buried underground and hardened and fossilized over millions to tens of millions of years.

Due to its beautiful coloring and transparency, amber has been used as decoration and gemstones since ancient times. While yellowish-brown is the most common color, it comes in various colors including red, blue, green, and black. Yellow and orange ones are particularly well-known.

Sometimes insects or plant fossils are trapped inside amber, which can increase its value even further.

Amber has been used not only as gemstones and decorations, but also historically as charms and medicine. Additionally, Estonia and the Baltic coastal countries are famous as some of the world's top amber-producing regions.

Regarding this amber,

I've created a setting where it holds

important elements in the world of "KUKU," FOMUS's character,

so I need to study amber as well,

and personally, Tallinn is a very educational place for me.

I also went to the cathedral.

Photography was prohibited inside, so here are just photos from the outside.

I also visited art galleries and craft shops,

and went to Raeapteek, the world's oldest pharmacy.

Raeapteek

The world's oldest pharmacy is Raeapteek, located in Tallinn, the capital of Estonia.

This pharmacy opened in 1422 and continues to operate to this day. Raeapteek is situated in the Old Town Hall Square of Tallinn, inside a historic building.

In its early days, the pharmacy not only compounded medicine but also sold herbs, medicinal alcohols, and even medicines made from unusual ingredients. It is said to have also dealt in products for health maintenance and beauty, not just treatment of illness.

Today, Raeapteek functions both as a pharmacy and as a museum, exhibiting historical medicines, tools, and compounded drugs. It is one of Estonia's tourist attractions, a place where you can feel the long history of the pharmacy.

After exploring a fair bit,

and since it started raining,

I headed back to the hostel.

I was planning to head out to a bar in the evening,

but the fatigue from yesterday (sleeping at the airport) had caught up with me,

so I took a short nap.

Then, after a light dinner (cup ramen),

I headed to the area where I'd been told there was a bar.

I'd heard that Estonian people gather about a 15-minute walk away, so I went to check it out,

but since it was Monday,

the bar scene was on the quieter side.

Still, I found an open bar

and drank at the counter.

Every time, I think to myself, when you go into a pub or bar alone,

the counter is really the only place to sit, haha.

But that's fine in its own way

since you can chat with the staff,

so it's not a problem, haha.

I had an IPA.

Despite it being Monday,

a decent number of people had gathered after 10 PM.

Since I have several important plans tomorrow,

I didn't stay too long and headed back to the hotel.

I took a shower and got into bed.

It's really nothing special, but

during this past month in Dublin,

the shower water temperature was low and it was pretty tough,

but the shower at this hotel in Tallinn

actually had warm water coming out, and

I was incredibly moved!!

In Japan, it's so taken for granted,

but being able to take a warm shower for the first time in a month

made me feel really happy.

Anyway, I'm going to push forward

with my activities in Tallinn tomorrow!

excerpt_en: 2024/10/22 So it's day 2 of my activities in Estonia. Today I jumped right into Masu Photo model shoots. Basically, the main way FOMUS recruits for Masu Photo shoots is through Instagram ads. Depending on the country, the number of people who show up varies a lot,
body_en:
2024/10/22

So it's day 2 of my activities in Estonia.

Today I jumped right into Masu Photo model shoots.

Basically, the main way FOMUS recruits for Masu Photo shoots

is through Instagram ads.

From my past activities, I already know that

the number of people who show up varies a lot by country,

so honestly, there's a gamble-like element to it,

but I started running ads two days ago

and I've been getting some messages,

and from those,

I've been selecting people who seem like a good fit

for FOMUS's Masu Photo and reaching out to them.

And so, it was time for the Masu Photo shoot!!

The first shoot in Estonia

So, the first shoot was with

Hellen.

She could actually speak a little Japanese!

She'd been to Japan once before,

and next year she'll be attending a university in Beppu for four years!!

I never expected to meet an Estonian who was so familiar with Japan right off the bat!

We mainly spoke in English during the shoot, but

since she could also speak Japanese,

we exchanged in both Japanese and English!

Plus, she had great energy,

and the shoot was really lively!

I don't think there could be a better vibe

for a Masu Photo shoot in Estonia.

It was fun from start to finish, on a level that made me think that.

And when that happens, the photo quality goes up too.

For Masu Photo, both the photographer and the model

need to be enjoying themselves,

otherwise you can't take the best Masu Photos, so

this shoot was absolutely wonderful!!

On top of that, she apparently participates in a Japanese language club in Tallinn, and the Japan-Estonia exchange event is happening tomorrow!

Once I heard that,

of course I wanted to go, haha!

So,

she sent me the link for it.

The continuation of this story is in the second half.

And so,

I got some wonderful Masu Photos.

Full throttle right from the start!

So, the Masu Photo shoots continued!!

The second shoot!

Shooting with Karina!

She looked relatively young,

and since it was a weekday,

I thought she might be a college student, but

when I asked, she was only 16!!

A high school student!

Apparently,

this week is autumn break in Estonia, so

school was off!

That's why she could participate.

Plus, this was her first time doing any kind of modeling!

She was very cool,

but when we talked, she was really fun,

and if I cracked a slightly humorous joke, she'd laugh,

so it was very rewarding to shoot with her!

She's still young, so I'm excited about her future,

and if she ever comes to Japan,

she's the kind of person I'd want to show around.

I mean, the fact that an Estonian high school student took interest in "Masu Photo" and participated in a shoot makes me so happy!!

Man, Estonia is the best, haha!

And on top of that,

I shot a third Masu Photo session,

but honestly, it wasn't like the first and second ones.

The fact that she wasn't Estonian

wasn't a problem at all, but

rather than being about Masu Photo,

I got the strong feeling that it was more like,

"I can get my photos taken for free!"

On top of that, she pulled out her own phone and

said "Please take photos of me without the masu,"

which honestly ticked me off,

so I shot for the minimum 40 minutes

and wrapped up the session quickly, haha.

Once my desire to shoot disappears,

the Masu Photo shoot doesn't work well.

"If you're a pro, you should do the job even if you don't feel like shooting" --

some might think that, but

I'm not getting paid by the models to shoot them,

this is a FOMUS project, and

I'm not going to bend over backwards to hype up someone who has no passion for Masu Photo.

*Unless she's really cute and I absolutely want to get great Masu Photos

I've shot in several countries so far,

and shoots where I can't feel good are rare, but

since I'm recruiting through Instagram ads

and shooting with limited information,

these kinds of problems do pop up from time to time.

I should probably screen more strictly, but this happened because I prioritized shooting more people over being selective about faces.

And so, today's Masu Photo shoots are done!

Overall,

it was a very interesting Masu Photo shoot day!

Off to the sauna!

So, after the shoots,

I dropped my camera off at the hostel,

grabbed a towel, and headed to an Estonian sauna!

Estonia is only a 2-hour ferry ride from Finland,

and it has a sauna culture.

I also heard from the models that

homes in Estonia come with saunas --

that's how famous and commonplace saunas are there.

So I'd been looking forward to the sauna.

There are several famous saunas,

and I decided to start with the cheapest one!

Today, I went to Raua Sauna, which costs 8 euros.

There were two types of sauna:

a Finnish sauna and a steam sauna.

Since it was a weekday,

there were few customers, so

it was practically a private session!

That said, there were a few people, and

I chatted with some older guys in the sauna.

It's overwhelmingly hotter than Japanese saunas,

and there aren't mats to sit on,

so you can't even sit in the sauna

unless you lay down your own towel, haha.

This really shows how Japanese saunas are

so much more considerate and thoughtful toward customers.

Plus, there's no cold plunge pool, so

you either use the shower,

or pull a rope that dumps stored cold water from a bucket overhead -- a manual bucket splash -- and cool down!

Well, that's what I'd like to say, but

there's no outdoor air bath either,

and you just stay indoors.

I was lying down on a bench.

I could do that because there were few people,

but on weekends, it might not be possible.

I alternated between sauna, steam, and

relaxation for about 2 hours,

and for the first time in a while, I was able to create a moment of total "emptiness."

Now that I think about it,

I hadn't been able to create time to zone out alone while overseas,

so it was good to get into a sauna at this timing.

And on the way back, I stopped by the kebab shop

for a post-sauna kebab,

dropped off my towel at the hostel, grabbed a masu,

and headed to a pub!! haha

Meetup in Tallinn

In the evening, I went to a meetup event in Tallinn!

I use a meetup app to host events in Ireland,

and when I searched for Tallinn,

there was an international exchange meetup

happening today,

and I had time to spare, so

I actually went.

I became friends with someone from Hungary on a short trip, chatted with a Spanish person who speaks Japanese,

and enjoyed interactions with people you can only meet at meetup events.

It started around 7:30,

and I left after 9.

The reason: it stopped being interesting.

*There weren't many people I could pitch FOMUS or MaSU to

*It wasn't just a drinking party for socializing

Then I headed to the second spot.

I'd been recommended a bar

with a huge selection of beers, so

I went to check it out.

Sure enough, there were tons of beer varieties!!

Since I love IPAs,

I asked for a recommendation and had one,

and it had a different taste than usual -- delicious!!

Here, instead of socializing, I drank quietly.

Going to different bars and pubs, each has its own vibe, so

it's quite educational.

Since I eventually want to host bar events myself,

spending time in these kinds of drinking spaces in foreign countries is full of lessons.

But there were just couples everywhere,

and unlike Japan, they're immediately kissing, so

I'm feeling "that's a cultural difference" and "nice..." haha.

So yeah,

today was another day of nonstop action.

I did 3 shoots, went to a sauna,

and hit up 2 bars at night -- active from morning to night,

full throttle.

My time in Estonia is limited to one week,

so just keeping moving is

the best approach for me.

Now, lastly -- I mentioned that the first model HELLEN

told me about a Japanese exchange event,

but unfortunately, that event isn't happening tomorrow; it's an Estonian cooking workshop instead.

She apologized, but

I was actually super interested in Estonian cuisine,

and when I heard there was a workshop where you can cook it,

I immediately reached out, and

there were still spots available

and anyone could participate, so

I booked it right away!!

So tomorrow, besides the shoot,

I've got an Estonian cooking workshop lined up too, haha!

This isn't something you'd normally find on your own,

so it's a connection that only happened because I did Masu Photo,

and using Masu Photo shoots as a reason

to interact with locals

is something regular tourists can't do.

Once again, I felt that the FOMUS project

is effective for connecting with people,

and moreover, if people other than me could also use this network, it would become even more interesting.

And as I was writing this article,

I ran out of beer, so that's it for now, haha!

Alright, doing my best again tomorrow!!

excerpt_en: So it's day 3 of activities in Estonia! Today I had 2 Masu Photo shoots again from the morning! The first was a high school student who wanted to try modeling. This time, due to scheduling constraints, it's been hard to shoot professional models, so I've been recruiting locals,
body_en:
So it's day 3 of activities in Estonia!

Today I had 2 Masu Photo shoots again from the morning!

The first was a high school student who wanted to try modeling.

This time, due to scheduling constraints, it's been hard to shoot professional models, so I've been recruiting locals, but for some reason, overwhelmingly young people are signing up.

Based on Instagram's policies, the ads should only be showing to people 19 and older,

but for some reason, the ones sending offers are 16! haha

That said, it's a chance for the younger generation to learn about FOMUS, so it's something to be grateful for.

So we started the shoot!

I've already been walking all over Tallinn's Old Town,

so I have a pretty good understanding

of the streets by now.

Plus, today is the first sunny day since I came to Tallinn!

Seizing the moment,

I snuck Masu-Game into my backpack as well.

*Masu-Game always appears in the Masu Photo photobook, so it needs to be properly photographed,

and I also want to leave room for it to be released as large-scale art pieces!

Whether I make a Masu Photo photobook for Estonia alone,

or include Latvia and Lithuania next year

and release it as a Baltic States edition,

I'm currently still thinking about it.

Since I had Masu-Game with me,

I made sure to photograph it properly!

What was funny was that

before shooting with the model,

when I was taking photos of Masu-Game,

several people asked me, "Hey! Are you traveling the world with that turtle?"

Maybe there are a lot of people who carry around items and take photos with them.

After the shoot, I had a bit of time, so

I went to a nearby food stall with an amazing atmosphere.

Here, for 5 euros,

they sell almonds coated with

caramel or sprinkled with cinnamon,

and they're perfect for a light snack.

Though I was surprised

at how big the portion was! haha

The second one!

On to the afternoon shoot!

This one was also a high school student! haha

I heard that this week

schools are on break in Estonia,

but talking to different people, it seems to vary.

*Either this week or next week is the break period

This shoot went great as well.

Off to school

Now, from here,

as I wrote in the previous post,

a model in Tallinn told me that

there's a weekly exchange event at a Japanese cafe.

However,

this week it wasn't an exchange event

but an Estonian cooking workshop!

I was way more interested in that

than a regular exchange event,

and although it was limited to 15 people,

I immediately sent a message and

managed to secure a spot.

That was yesterday's story.

So today was cooking day!

The participants included

Ukrainians, locals,

and Japanese students studying abroad in Estonia.

There were working adults, university students,

and even high school students -- I was surprised!!

And so, my first cooking class in my life

ended up being in Estonia, haha.

First, we got a talk about the history of Estonian cuisine,

ate some dishes that had been prepared in advance,

and then the cooking workshop started!

The steps weren't too many,

so we cooperated as a group.

It's hard to find a restaurant

that serves Estonian cuisine,

so actually making it ourselves like this

was a great experience.

Plus, I got to talk with

university and high school students studying abroad in Estonia,

and knowing that there are people out there working hard

around the world was a big learning experience for me too.

In particular, there was someone who came from Okinawa as a first-year high school student,

and they had all sorts of things they wanted to do,

so they asked me for advice

on how to make those happen.

I still have plenty of things I want to do myself,

and there are more things I haven't accomplished,

but in terms of making a living from what I love,

I'm managing somehow, so

I offered a little bit of advice.

That said, what I told them was,

"Build your own fanbase."

The key is to just keep building trust!

That's the gist of it.

And so,

I got to learn about Estonian cuisine

and meet Japanese students studying in Estonia,

making it a very meaningful time.

If I hadn't done Masu Photo in Estonia,

I would never have even known

about this workshop,

so when you keep taking action,

something else always comes along.

Indeed, taking action is important!!

After the workshop,

I hit up 2 more bars.

I even went to a bar where you can't get in without a passcode.

*I'd been told the code in advance, so I got in.

title_en: The Local Sauna and Bars Are Insane!
excerpt_en: So it's day 4 of my stay in Estonia. No shoots today, so I spent the daytime working on FOMUS tasks and headed to the sauna in the evening! There are 3 famous saunas in Tallinn, and I want to hit them all! I went to a different one from last time,
body_en:
So it's day 4 of my stay in Estonia.

No shoots today, so

I spent the daytime working on FOMUS tasks

and headed to the sauna in the evening!

There are 3 famous saunas in Tallinn,

and I want to hit them all if I can!

I went to a different one from last time --

"KALMA SAUNA."

Despite it being a weekday, it was 15 euros!

Super expensive!! haha

This sauna had a cold plunge pool, but

honestly, it wasn't cold at all! haha

The sauna was insanely hot!

And naturally,

everyone except me was a local older guy,

so we chatted during the sauna.

What's different from Japan is that they bring a vihta (birch branch bundle) into the sauna and whack their bodies with it.

Also, they just casually talk.

And what really felt like "this is overseas!" was that

during sauna breaks, everyone hangs out on a sofa in the locker room.

On the table during that hangout: grilled fish and beer.

They're basically having a drinking party at the sauna!!

In Japan, you might eat or have beer after a sauna, but these guys bring their own food and eat there, haha.

Plus, they sell beer right in the sauna changing room! haha

No Oropo here, obviously.

Then, after going into the sauna about 4 times,

just as I was about to leave,

I got called over -- "Hey, Japan!"

When I went into the sauna,

they said, "I brought some mint, so I'm going to do a loyly!"

And they poured mint extract on the stones --

a self-service scented loyly! haha

Anything goes here!

It's so different from Japan,

so it's perfect for cultural exchange and experiencing different cultures!

I was able to enjoy all of it!

But still, 15 euros is too expensive!! haha

Invited to a bar

During this Estonia trip,

a few friends introduced me to people living in Tallinn.

Tiny, whom I met in Los Angeles,

introduced me to a Hong Kong guy living in Tallinn,

but he was currently back in Hong Kong,

so he introduced me to a friend of his who lives in Tallinn.

I'd messaged that guy,

and he replied before my sauna today, saying

"I'm drinking in Tallinn's Old Town tonight -- want to join?"

An invitation!

Of course, I went to drink.

We met up at a sports bar

and cheers'd with 3 people!

Apparently, only on Thursdays, Heineken is 2 euros -- super cheap!

This is because the sports bar pairs it with betting,

hence the price.

Lure them in with Heineken, get them betting.

What a brilliant business model, haha.

I don't gamble,

so I just drank beer normally,

and when they ordered shots,

I made sure to keep up.

I drank a ton, somehow.

Plus 2 more beers on top of that.

The bartender was also a very beautiful woman.

And then, on to the next bar.

I was basically just following along.

We went to an underground hidden bar.

There, the bartender started making some mysterious cocktails, and everyone cheers'd!

From around this point, my memory gets fuzzy,

but judging from photos, I had at least 3 drinks.

Incredibly strong ones.

And so,

my limiter hit its breaking point

and I automatically went into "head home" mode!

*In my case, when I've had too much and need to get home, I unconsciously start heading back

My memory is hazy, but I made it back to the hostel alone and went to sleep.

I didn't pay at either the first or second bar.

I trust that the guys I was drinking with covered it.

*They were very welcoming, so I let myself be treated

When they come to Japan, I'll show them around.

And so,

this was the day I learned firsthand

that trying to keep up with the locals

will absolutely wreck you, haha!

excerpt_en: So today I started with an online meeting for FOMUS's Japan projects in the morning, then spent the rest of the time working alone. By the way, I also moved to a different hotel. *Because I want to see different hotels. When all is said and done,
body_en:
So today I started with an online meeting for FOMUS's Japan projects in the morning,

then spent the rest of the time working alone.

By the way, I also moved to a different hotel.

*Because I want to see different hotels

When all is said and done,

having a full day to myself in good health

has been rare since I came to Ireland,

so I had time to think about various things.

I love working with others,

and drinking and chatting is fun too,

but I also need time alone to just face myself right now.

There's so much daily input that

I want to properly output it,

and I need time to input

what I want to input.

So,

since I had free time in Estonia,

I watched SoftBank's Masayoshi Son's

lecture about AI.

He gives a talk about technology every year, and I remember going to hear one when I was a student.

Indeed, the evolution of AI is incredible,

and seeing Son passionately speaking

made me feel like I need to engage more with AI too!!

After finishing the lecture,

I actually worked with ChatGPT

to think through FOMUS's next strategies.

At this point, it's o1, so

AI has already reached the stage of "thinking,"

and before watching this lecture, I was using GPT-4,

but after hearing the talk,

I tried out o1.

I consulted on the core aspects of FOMUS,

and through an incredible amount of computation,

I was able to refine new strategies for FOMUS.

Normally, you'd bring in a business consultant for this kind of thing,

but with just AI, it can think through the problem with me

and give me answers right away,

making it incredibly productive!

So,

over these past few weeks,

I was able to build the foundation for

FOMUS's subscription-based community.

This is planned for release after November,

and it's about bringing all the people

who've been connected through me and FOMUS

into one proper community.

There will be monthly or annual fees,

but you can connect with people worldwide,

and since only people who've been involved with FOMUS or MaSU would find value in joining,

in a way, it'll be a gathering of core people.

A community for Japanese traditional crafts,

where I've used my own two feet more than anyone,

sat down face-to-face with each person,

not just in Japan but overseas too --

I'd love for the people I've met along the way

to join as well,

and I can introduce this FOMUS subscription

to people I meet in the future too,

so the benefits are plentiful!

Honestly, the value exceeds the price,

and I can put the services I've built over these 3 years at FOMUS into it,

and members who sign up

can also do skill sharing,

meaning they can sell their strengths and skills.

Furthermore, if job matching happens within the FOMUS community, both parties earn points, and I think in the future those points can be exchanged for skill shares, products, or services.

Also,

I've launched a masu sharing service,

and if you become a member, you can use it for just the shipping cost, which alone would pay for the membership fee.

The sharing service is Japan-only at first,

but once it gets on track, it can expand globally,

and if even one person in each country

is willing to store masu,

the masu sharing can happen worldwide.

*Could even franchise it?

Also, I went to the Philippines to study English, and the online English classes from teachers I met there can also be made available exclusively to FOMUS subscription members,

which would help improve the English skills of Japanese members.

Among the tons of online English conversation services out there,

there'd be an English class you can only take if you're in the FOMUS community! haha

This would help block out sketchy people,

reduce risk for the teachers,

and most importantly, if the English skills of people involved with FOMUS improve, it'll be useful when we go overseas together in the future!

Of course, I'll use it too.

The online English conversation service alone

seems like it could pay for the FOMUS subscription.

In the future,

if we could also create study sessions for overseas members to learn Japanese, it would be amazing if FOMUS's international members could speak Japanese when they visit Japan.

Also, most people who find me and FOMUS interesting love Japan, so we can match them with Japanese members, and I can introduce the guesthouses I stay at,

creating a win-win relationship for everyone.

By making the FOMUS community

subscription-based, members will have to pay a little,

but in return, I can use that money to expand services,

so I can also increase their satisfaction.

Above all, until now I've been sustaining FOMUS

on my own earnings alone,

but the scale has been gradually growing

and investment amounts have been increasing,

so a stable subscription income

has become a necessity.

To stabilize the business even a little,

transitioning to a subscription community was inevitable.

I'd been thinking about the subscription system and its internals

all by myself,

but with AI as an amazing support,

I was able to flesh out the details much more.

Having time to think about these things

requires being alone,

and in Dublin it was hard to get time alone, so coming to Estonia was the right call.

Plus, thinking about stuff like this requires

a decent environment (a quiet, focused space), and Tallinn's Old Town has tons of cafes, so I found a stylish cafe that stays open late and spent the whole time having dialogues with AI there.

I spent over 5 hours at this cafe,

but I only paid about 1,600 yen.

I could focus, and I got to eat cake too, so

it didn't feel expensive.

Moreover, during this time,

I was also running ads on FOMUS's Instagram to recruit Estonian models [final push],

so I was also handling communication with model candidates in parallel.

About 10 people messaged me,

but since the condition was that I could only shoot "tomorrow and the day after," not many could do it.

Still, one person was confirmed, so

it was worth keeping the ads running until the last minute,

and switching to the images of the model I shot first

to show that I'm actually shooting in Tallinn was also effective.

Switching to the local model's photo boosted credibility

And so,

today was a day of nonstop work.

Days like these are important too!!

Alright, tomorrow is another Masu Photo shoot!

Let's do this!

title_en: I Met a Talented Young Person
excerpt_en: Day 6 of my stay in Estonia. Yesterday I rested my body for the day, so I'm feeling great! *Though my brain was running at full throttle. So today, Masu Photo shoots from the morning! This was the person whose shoot was confirmed yesterday. She's 23,
body_en:
Day 6 of my stay in Estonia.

Yesterday I rested my body for the day, so

I'm feeling great!

*Though my brain was running at full throttle

So today, Masu Photo shoots from the morning!

This was the person whose shoot was confirmed yesterday.

She's 23,

and currently not working due to health issues, and she loves international exchange,

so she was interested in FOMUS's Masu Photo, and apparently her cousin married a Japanese person.

My skill as a photographer

in terms of actual shooting technique is decent.

But my skill in making the atmosphere fun and getting the subject to relax during a shoot -- I think that's top-notch.

Until now, I've been honing this skill to the max in Japan, but this year I need to apply it to work with people worldwide,

and I went to language schools in the Philippines and Ireland

for 3 months each.

I also changed my living environment,

put effort into increasing time spent speaking English,

and I've gotten to a level where I can communicate reasonably well in English, including cracking jokes.

It helps that during photo shoots,

we don't get into heavy topics! haha

She and I got into a conversation about anime

and it was really fun.

In that flow,

I also told her about FOMUS's character KUKU,

and she was very supportive!

And so,

I got lots of great photos.

Next up, the second person!

She's a student

who wants to work in fashion.

So she's been learning posing and such,

and her transitions are really smooth!

She's also interested in international exchange and culture,

which is why she participated this time!

Indeed, the combination of "Japanese traditional crafts x photography"

is strong as something that's never been done before!

*It clicks with certain people

What I keep realizing is that

the locals who come as Masu Photo models

are all really interesting!!

They're all young, so

I can't talk business with them though! haha

And then, the third person.

I found a talent.

She's 16,

and sent me a message asking "Is it possible to do a shoot?"

Of course it was okay, so

I told her there wouldn't be any accessory promotion,

but I'd love for her to participate,

and that's how this shoot came about.

Asian people tend to look younger,

while European people look much more mature, but this person looked young even by appearance!

It was her first time modeling,

so she was quite nervous,

but putting her at ease is also my role.

After doing a light shoot,

we went into a nearby cafe

and prioritized communication!

We talked about various things,

and it turned out she's an artist.

She told me her work is exhibited in a gallery, and she showed me photos.

She can also draw illustrations,

and the quality is high!!

Since I'm also involved in art-related work,

I felt this girl has incredible potential,

so I gave her one of my remaining "FOMUS masu" --

I don't have many left.

Use this masu

and create whatever art you want!

That was my request.

I don't know what she'll become yet, but

what I can do in moments like these

is create opportunities.

I'm also running an art gallery project behind the scenes, and if she becomes famous, maybe we can collaborate there.

If, 5 years from now,

her work is exhibited at the FOMUS gallery

and someone asks,

"How did you get connected with FOMUS?"

the answer would be:

"In October 2024, MaSU visited Estonia for just one week, we connected through Masu Photo, and he gave me a single masu. That was the beginning."

If a story like that could be born, that would be amazing.

Of course, things don't always work out this nicely,

but if you don't take action, nothing starts,

and I thought it would be cool if this became one of those sparks.

She already speaks Russian and Estonian,

and is learning English,

plus she does ballet, dance, and more,

and what surprised me was that

she'd even done a bit of kendo.

I couldn't believe how many things she's interested in and doing at such a young age!!

When I was in high school,

all I did was kendo and Pokemon,

so meeting someone with such a promising future

through Masu Photo was wonderful!

FOMUS's concept of

"connecting through masu" and "expressing yourself through masu"

is embodied through Masu Photo.

If even when someone other than me takes Masu Photos,

a system emerges where community members feel grateful -- that would be absolutely amazing.

I digressed a bit, but

it was an incredibly interesting day.

And I edited all the photos from the models

I shot over the past few days

plus today's 3 people in one go!!

Tomorrow's model shoot fell through, so

this marks the end of Masu Photo shoots in Estonia!

In total, I was able to shoot 8 people.

Moreover, 7 of them were Estonian,

making this the highest match rate

between nationality and shooting location so far.

So since tomorrow is now a free day,

I contacted HELLEN, who was my first model,

and we've made plans to go to a cafe in the afternoon!

Until then I'm free, so

I plan to explore and do some last sightseeing in Tallinn!

excerpt_en: Day 7 in Estonia! Since my flight is in the morning tomorrow, today is essentially the last day! This one week went by incredibly fast. With all the Masu Photo shoots in Estonia finished, this morning I went alone to a spot overlooking Tallinn's Old Town (where I walk every day) to photograph Masu-Game.
body_en:
Day 7 in Estonia!

Since my flight is in the morning tomorrow, today is essentially the last day!

This one week went by incredibly fast.

With all the Masu Photo shoots in Estonia finished,

this morning I went alone to a spot overlooking Tallinn's Old Town (where I walk every day) to photograph Masu-Game.

When I'm with models, I can't really

shoot Masu-Game,

so I took my time shooting when I was alone.

The weather was cloudy,

but I got some great photos.

Then I headed back to the hotel

to work on FOMUS tasks.

When I happened to visit a coworking space,

I met a Japanese person.

She works for a Japanese company in a fully remote position and is traveling the world while working.

I never expected to meet someone like that in Estonia.

It's been a while since I met another person traveling without a home, haha.

I told her about my activities,

and she found them really interesting.

Indeed, the combination of masu x photography

is something only I'm doing in the world, so

in a way, it has rarity.

Though the demand is also pretty small, haha.

But when you add a community on top of that,

the demand surges.

In today's world, belonging to a community outside of family and work actually has surprising demand.

Of course, there are all kinds of communities out there,

so not all of them are great,

but the FOMUS community has a system where

you connect through having a masu

or expressing yourself through masu,

so people interested in that will naturally gather.

It won't suddenly be a community

with 100 members or anything,

but rather one that grows gradually.

And it'll include users from both Japan and overseas.

I considered adding Japanese users first and creating the overseas group later,

but if I go with "once the Japanese format is set,"

knowing myself, I'd probably force-fit the overseas group into that format,

so I decided to run both simultaneously,

taking the best from each

while running the community.

Also, the FOMUS community

is not an online salon, so

it's not necessarily a place where you learn things.

But just by being in it,

you can connect with people not just in Japan but worldwide,

meet people you wouldn't normally meet,

and there's FOMUS as a common topic,

so how you approach it is up to each person.

I didn't go into all this detail,

but I did talk about the community.

Also, she likes drawing, so

she drew a portrait of me.

She drew it in about 10 minutes.

The masu looks great!!

Then I had to move to my next appointment.

Connecting through masu

I went to a cafe with Hellen,

the first masu model I shot in Estonia.

During the Masu Photo shoot, she told me about a Japanese language school cafe (which turned out to lead me to the cooking class instead), and I was able to have an experience that's hard to come by.

She's also starting at a university in Beppu next year,

so she can speak decent Japanese.

And for a 22-year-old,

she's incredibly mature

and sees a lot of things clearly.

That's why I thought it'd be fun to have a long chat with her,

and I invited her to a cafe.

As it turned out,

it was incredibly enjoyable talking with her.

I also told her about FOMUS's upcoming projects, and she found them really interesting and agreed to participate!

When the things FOMUS can offer increase,

the people who want to join also increase.

This is truly powerful.

The strength of a community

If I hadn't been running the FOMUS Masu Photo project

in Estonia at this timing,

we never would have met, so

luck and timing are extremely important.

If I'd gone somewhere other than Estonia,

these encounters might not have happened.

Also, the people I'm currently connected with in Japan

would have a hard time building connections

with locals living overseas.

But,

from my own experience, I know very well that

life is more fun when you have friends all over the world!!

If that's the case,

being that bridge is my greatest role,

and by actually going to places and connecting,

or meeting people through the Masu Photo project,

I can bring them together in the FOMUS community.

That way, I can facilitate mutual bridging, and

"I was able to meet this person because of the FOMUS community"

-- that becomes the experience.

This kind of experience is only possible

in the FOMUS community,

and no other Japanese community can create this kind of connection.

There are plenty of global communities,

and there are anime-focused groups too,

so there are countless places to connect through hobbies in the modern world.

But FOMUS and MaSU

can become a hub (a gathering place)

and connect people.

It's a bit unique --

not just hobbies, but your strengths and skills

might be exactly what someone else is looking for,

so being able to share skills

is something unique to the FOMUS community.

I think that's the differentiating element.

Additionally, we plan to implement a token economy in the future, so approaches that only FOMUS -- which covers both "Japanese traditional crafts x cutting-edge technology" -- can take will also become possible.

I've been thinking about all this stuff nonstop

during my time in Estonia.

For me, this Estonia stay

somehow felt like it was meant to be.

I had so many encounters,

and of course, I didn't just want them to end as one-time meetings -- I worked to make people want to stay connected long-term.

For that, what's important is how quickly

you can get people to know you,

and communication is truly crucial.

If FOMUS members can work together

to build this up,

it'll become something even more amazing,

and I plan to regularly hold online international exchange events.

That said, there's the language barrier,

so I'll need to think through that carefully! haha

Summary

Over this past week, I met various people through Masu Photo, and the experience in Estonia in particular became a catalyst for discovering new possibilities for FOMUS.

I'm convinced that FOMUS will grow not just as a project for masu photography and traditional craft promotion, but as a community hub that connects people around the world.

Well then, tomorrow I'm heading back to Ireland.

And in less than a week,

I'll be traveling through Malta, Greece, and Cyprus -- 3 countries.

It'd be amazing if I can do Masu Photo shoots there too!

For my last night in Tallinn, I went to a beer bar.

excerpt_en: So, I headed to the airport in the morning. My flight was at 11:50, and I arrived at Tallinn Airport about 3 hours early. There are two reasons for going early: first, I absolutely cannot miss the flight. And second, I wanted to experience the paid lounge.
body_en:
So, I headed to the airport in the morning.

My flight was at 11:50, and

I arrived at Tallinn Airport about 3 hours early.

There are two reasons for going early:

First,

I absolutely cannot miss the flight.

And second,

I wanted to experience the paid lounge.

I have a Priority Pass that lets me

enter airport lounges around the world, so

I can get into any lounge for free

except for airline-exclusive VIP lounges.

Of course,

some of them serve alcohol, so

I can drink,

and I can eat light meals too.

So,

I quickly got through the luggage check

and headed to the lounge.

I was the only one there, haha.

It was incredibly empty.

The Tallinn Airport lounge

didn't have a huge variety of food, but it had a salad bar, which was a godsend for me since I've been lacking vegetables recently!

Hot dishes started at noon, apparently,

so unfortunately I couldn't have any.

Still, Estonia's beer "SAKU"

was all-you-can-drink.

Maybe because I've been drinking

so much Guinness in Dublin,

it tasted quite light to me.

But it was still delicious.

I still had plenty of time, so

I started working on a promotional video

featuring the masu models from this Estonia trip.

That said,

it was basically a slideshow of the photos I took.

Rather than trying to get views,

I wanted it to serve as a portfolio

for both me and the models,

and that's why I made the video.

This is also a new challenge for me.

Taking local models shot in the same country

and putting them into a single video right after shooting,

and on top of that,

not a single professional model is in this one.

The person with the most influence

is essentially MaSU,

so I'll have to wait a few days

to see what kind of results this brings.

But I'm sure it'll have a positive effect,

because for the upcoming shoots in

Malta, Greece, and Cyprus,

model candidates will definitely watch this video

when I do the same kind of promotion.

Those who resonate with it

will participate regardless of the country.

As a promotional video that speaks to

potential models,

I think it's quite powerful.

It'd be amazing if this could lead to online masu purchases

or large orders,

but since it's a Masu Photo project,

it's hard to create a pipeline that leads there.

In any country,

if I could showcase through cooking events

that masu can also serve as tableware

and make a video of it,

I feel like FOMUS's masu would spread even further.

Anyway,

I was making a video in the most amazing lounge, haha.

Schedule from here

So, I'm heading back to Dublin,

where I'll be doing activities in Ireland for about 5 days.

That said, the purpose is

to experience Halloween in Ireland.

Halloween originated in Ireland, and

I really wanted to see it.

In the future, I want to plan a Halloween x masu (FOMUS) event,

whether to take a cosplay-style Halloween approach like Japan, or do a Halloween cooking event --

I have ideas, but I just don't have enough options in my head yet.

About 2 or 3 years ago, I collaborated with a chef who works with FOMUS to plan a Halloween cooking event, and while it succeeded as a proper cooking event,

it didn't lead to community expansion

or business connections.

So, while connecting people,

I want to create a Halloween event

that makes people become FOMUS fans

from 2025 onward,

but at the moment I haven't come up with anything,

so over the next few days,

I plan to absorb whatever I can.

Then, from November 3rd, I'll be in Malta.

After that, Athens in Greece,

then Cyprus -- about 20 days total,

doing Masu Photo shoots

and gathering people who resonate with FOMUS.

Also, from November onward,

I think I'll be able to announce this,

but I'm planning to create a ceiling art piece for an art gallery opening in 2025 on Sagi Island, involving people from all over the world.

That includes people from this Estonia trip --

I want people from all sorts of countries to participate.

So even during this trip,

I'm also simultaneously looking for artists who will work on the ceiling art with me.

From the outside,

my activities might look like I'm traveling the world, but most of my time is spent expanding the FOMUS brand, and the time I spend on pure sightseeing without any work purpose is zero.

Of course, I do visit famous tourist spots.

That's because they become venues for Masu Photo shoots,

and moreover,

to make the world of FOMUS's character "KUKU" as amazing as possible, I need to experience those worlds myself or they won't become options for me, so I keep moving from place to place.

The reason I included Greece this time is because

the atmosphere of the Acropolis temples

seems like it could be incorporated into KUKU's world,

so I'm actually going to see it in person.

Like this,

having a base in Ireland

allows me to travel around Europe aggressively.

If I were flying to each country from Japan,

the cost and time would be several times more.

By traveling the world at this timing,

connecting with locals,

and getting them to know about FOMUS,

the number of allies grows.

Plus, FOMUS is still developing

and incomplete in many ways,

and I can show people how it gradually takes shape

through the community and SNS,

which also creates support and roles for everyone.

I want to support the challenges of those who join the community, and I want to grow together,

so I want to make sure to have both the real-world and online environments covered!!

Arriving in Dublin

After returning to Dublin,

I headed to Taiki-san's house where I'll be staying for these 5 days.

Right now,

I don't have a home in Ireland either, so

I'm completely freeloading, haha.

That said, I've been living like this

in both Japan and overseas for over 3 years,

so there's no problem at all.

I'm fully taking advantage of human connections.

Of course, I'll work hard to repay them even more,

and I'll show it through my actions.

excerpt_en: So, since coming to Ireland, this is my first weekday with nothing going on! So I started with a FOMUS meeting in the morning, then spent the rest of the time working alone. Since I've had time to think by myself these past few days, I've been able to do a lot of input and output,
body_en:
So, since coming to Ireland,

this is my first weekday with nothing going on!

So,

I started with a FOMUS meeting in the morning,

then spent the rest of the time working alone.

Since I've had time to think by myself these past few days,

I've been able to do a lot of input and output,

my head feels refreshed, and

I spent the whole time thinking about

how to make the FOMUS subscription service enjoyable

and how to make each person feel like

FOMUS is their own thing.

My conclusion was: "Create white space."

The key is

making people feel "It's okay for me to be in this community!"

and if you leave white space,

everyone will find their own role and take action.

For those who want to be given a role,

I'll give them one and let them act.

It's not about which is better or worse --

it's about strengths and weaknesses,

so that's up to each individual.

It'd be amazing if everyone could have a role

and work toward expanding FOMUS.

I spent the day thinking up strategies for building those kinds of self-driven FOMUS members.

What I'm currently considering is

making it an invitation-only system.

For November, I might make it invitation-only,

creating a flow where "you can't join unless a core member invites you."

This would help build up

the desire to participate.

It's a system like Clubhouse, which was popular a while back.

However, the weakness is that

if someone asks whether the content and services are polished enough to generate that much enthusiasm like Clubhouse did, that's questionable.

FOMUS is a community,

a place where people meet through masu, so

at first, I'll take the lead in reaching out,

and gradually grow the community from there.

Well, the actual format is still undecided, but I want to launch in November,

so I'd like to finalize this soon.

Then, I had another online meeting in the afternoon.

The topic of the subscription came up,

and someone asked, "Aren't you going to do member numbers?"

I hadn't been planning to.

Because with a monthly subscription,

even if someone joins early and gets a low number, if they quit,

that number disappears.

Early numbers would increase in value over time, so

if I were to implement member numbers,

my honest wish is to give them to

existing FOMUS core members first.

Whether to implement this is still under consideration.

If I hadn't had that online meeting,

I wouldn't have had this idea at all,

so talking with someone is indeed important.

It was a very educational time.

FOMUS's core members are the kind of people

who give each other

those kinds of insights,

so talking with them is always incredibly fun.

So,

I spent the day building out those systems.

I started work at 7 AM

and before I knew it, it was 4 PM.

I'd been in the room the whole time, so

I decided it was time to go outside and headed to the city.

I felt like drinking with someone,

so I messaged Arisa, a Japanese woman I can casually grab drinks with, and she was already in the city,

so I met up with her and we went for drinks.

One-on-one conversations with her

have a great balance of talking and listening,

making it a very meaningful time.

Spending all day at the computer doing work I love is super fun, but

going out for drinks and outputting

is also important.

After one drink, we walked around a little to see if any Halloween events were happening,

but unfortunately there was nothing going on, haha.

Temple Bar was already in Christmas mode, haha.

It was a very productive work day!
